questions.q: In a cathode-ray tube, which electrode are the cathode rays emitted from, and what is the sign of its potential?
options: The cathode, which is connected to the negative terminal
The anode, which is connected to the positive terminal
The cathode, which is connected to the positive terminal
The anode, which is connected to the negative terminal
questions.explanation: Cathode rays originate at the cathode — the negative electrode. The shadow experiments confirm this because the shadow always points away from the cathode. The anode is the positive electrode the rays travel toward; writing that the rays are 'emitted by the anode' is a common error.

questions.q: Why must a discharge tube be operated at low gas pressure?
options: Low pressure makes the glass fluoresce more brightly
At low pressure the gas particles are far apart, giving a long mean free path so the electrons accelerate to high speed and cross the tube without losing energy in collisions
Low pressure increases the charge on each electron
At high pressure the magnetic field cannot penetrate the tube
questions.explanation: At low pressure the gas particles are spread out, so the mean free path is long and the electrons can accelerate the full length of the tube without colliding away their energy. At high pressure they would collide almost immediately and never reach the far end.

questions.q: Which sequence correctly 'accounts for' the origin of cathode rays?
options: The anode heats up, boils off electrons, and the field pulls them to the cathode
The glowing gas physically flows from the cathode to the anode
A strong electric field ionises the low-pressure gas and frees electrons, which the field then accelerates from the negative cathode toward the positive anode
Photons from the fluorescent glass push electrons back toward the cathode
questions.explanation: The strong field from the high potential difference ionises the gas and liberates electrons from the cathode, then accelerates those free negative electrons toward the anode — the accelerated electrons are the cathode rays. The glow is just gas atoms lighting up where the electrons pass; it is the electrons, not the gas, that travel.

questions.q: A Maltese cross placed in the beam casts a sharp shadow on the fluorescent glass. What does this most directly demonstrate?
options: The rays carry negative charge
The rays travel in straight lines and are blocked by solid objects
The rays have mass
The rays travel at the speed of light
questions.explanation: A sharp, well-defined shadow can only form if the rays travel in straight lines and are stopped by a solid opaque object. (The shadow pointing away from the cathode is bonus evidence the rays come from the cathode.) Charge and mass come from the field and paddle-wheel experiments, not the cross.

questions.q: The paddle wheel rolls along its rails away from the cathode, even slightly uphill. According to the syllabus interpretation, this is evidence that cathode rays:
options: are electromagnetic waves like light
carry positive charge
carry momentum and kinetic energy, and therefore have mass
travel slower than the speed of sound
questions.explanation: To roll a wheel and do mechanical work against friction and gravity, the rays must transfer momentum and kinetic energy — evidence they are particles with mass, not a massless wave. (Modern analysis adds heating effects, but the marks come from the momentum/mass interpretation.)

questions.q: When cathode rays pass between charged parallel plates, the beam deflects toward the positive plate. The conclusion that earns the mark is that the rays:
options: are attracted to the positive plate
carry negative charge
carry no charge
move at constant speed
questions.explanation: A charge attracted toward the positive plate must itself be negative, so the conclusion is that cathode rays carry negative charge. Stopping at 'attracted to the positive plate' only restates the observation — the mark is in the word 'negative'.

questions.q: A magnetic field curves the cathode-ray beam into a circular arc but does not deflect light at all. Why is the deflection of the beam significant beyond just showing the rays are charged?
options: It proves the rays are a form of light
It shows the rays are uncharged but moving
Because a magnet does not deflect light, the deflection is independent evidence that cathode rays are not simply light
It shows the rays travel at the speed of light
questions.explanation: A magnetic field only exerts a force on moving charges, and light is not deflected by a magnet. So the beam curving is independent evidence that the rays are charged moving particles, not electromagnetic radiation. The circular path arises because the magnetic force stays perpendicular to the velocity, acting as a centripetal force.

questions.q: An electron of charge \(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C}\) moves at \(2.5 \times 10^{4}\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\) perpendicular to a magnetic field of \(2.0 \times 10^{-2}\ \mathrm{T}\). Using \(F = qvB\), the force on it is:
questions.explanation: \(F = qvB = (1.6\times10^{-19})(2.5\times10^{4})(2.0\times10^{-2})\). The front numbers give \(1.6\times2.5\times2.0 = 8.0\) and the powers of ten give \((-19)+4+(-2) = -17\), so \(F = 8.0 \times 10^{-17}\ \mathrm{N}\).

questions.q: Early experiments showed no deflection of cathode rays in an electric field, which misled some toward a wave model. What did Thomson identify as the true cause?
options: Cathode rays really are uncharged
The plates were too far apart to create a field
Residual gas in the imperfectly evacuated tube was ionised, and the resulting ions drifted to the plates and neutralised them, cancelling the electric field
The magnetic field of the Earth cancelled the deflection
questions.explanation: The tube was not evacuated well enough; the rays ionised the leftover gas and those ions neutralised the charged plates, cancelling the field — so no deflection occurred. With a better vacuum the field was no longer neutralised and the beam deflected toward the positive plate. The rays were negatively charged all along; the apparatus, not the physics, was at fault.

questions.q: Considering all four classic experiments together, which pairing of observations gives evidence of mass versus negative charge?
options: Maltese cross shows mass; magnetic field shows charge
Paddle wheel shows mass; electric- and magnetic-field deflection show negative charge
Electric field shows mass; paddle wheel shows charge
Magnetic field shows mass; Maltese cross shows charge
questions.explanation: The paddle wheel (momentum and work) is the evidence for mass, while deflection toward the positive plate in an electric field and the negative-charge curvature in a magnetic field are the evidence for negative charge. The Maltese cross is supporting evidence for straight-line, particle-stream travel.
